text: I'm sharing a R-tap for a right direct hernia. Very symptomatic for this gentleman. Robotic tap has become my more comfortable way of fixing these hernias, although there are certainly many other options.
The surgeon creates a flap in the pre-transversalis plane, focusing on medial dissection first for ease, while working above the arcuate line.

The surgeon begins lateral dissection, searching for the cord structures which come into view. The vas deferens is visible, and the peritoneum is already retracted. The surgeon gently sweeps the cord structures down while following the dissection plane.
The surgeon dissects the medial surface of the direct hernia to create space, followed by the reduction of the hernia, which is visually easier compared to indirect hernias.

The surgeon dissects to the contralateral side past the midline in a direct hernia repair, creating space for mesh placement. Fat is carefully removed from the vessels and pubic sidewall to ensure the mesh sits flat.
The surgeon approaches the operative space, revealing a noticeable fat plug commonly found in this area.
The surgeon parietalizes the cord structures, checking for a lipoma and reducing it sufficiently to ensure it remains on the parietal surface of the mesh.

The surgeon imbricates the hernia sac using a suture, ensuring tissue coverage over the defect to prevent mesh eventration. The sac is manipulated with silk sutures, although other techniques like V-lock sutures or tacking to the abdominal wall are mentioned as alternatives.
The surgeon places a 16x12 anatomic mesh more medially to ensure coverage over the direct hernia site. The mesh is positioned to sit flat, covering the obturator and femoral spaces.
The surgeon secures the mesh medially over the direct hernia defect to prevent potential migration. The lipoma is visible beyond the edge of the mesh. The surgical team proceeds to close the flap.

The surgeon runs the camera back to inspect the dissected space. An angiocath is placed into the flap and connected to suction, visibly evacuating gas to ensure the mesh and surrounding tissues are flat.
